supporting_evidence: Clear history of mechanical fall at home — fell and hit head on floor
Age 81 with osteoporosis and hypertension — high baseline fall risk
Multiple medications including enalapril (orthostatic risk) and warfarin (balance impairment not expected but polypharmacy contributes to fall risk)
Stable vital signs and GCS 15 support uncomplicated mechanical fall as most likely primary event
Head strike + warfarin anticoagulation is the classic high-risk combination for subdural hematoma
Age-related cerebral atrophy stretches bridging veins, increasing vulnerability to shearing forces
Subdural hematoma classically presents with lucid interval — patient may appear well initially before deteriorating
Dizziness may be an early sign of developing intracranial pathology
NICE CG176: all patients on anticoagulants with head injury require CT head within 1 hour regardless of GCS
81-year-old female with osteoporosis reporting hip pain after fall — textbook presentation
Hip fracture incidence peaks in women aged 80-85 with osteoporosis
Low-energy fall in osteoporotic bone is sufficient mechanism
Occult hip fractures may have normal initial X-rays — MRI sensitivity is >99%
Warfarin complicates surgical management if fracture requires fixation

candidates.diagnosis: Pre-syncopal fall secondary to cardiac arrhythmia
supporting_evidence: Known atrial fibrillation — paroxysmal AF can cause transient cerebral hypoperfusion
Dizziness could represent the pre-syncopal event that caused the fall rather than a consequence of it
Enalapril can potentiate orthostatic hypotension, particularly in elderly with reduced baroreceptor sensitivity
Must perform ECG to assess current rhythm and consider Holter monitoring
If syncope caused the fall, recurrence risk is high and requires separate management
Head strike mechanism involves cervical spine axial loading and potential hyperextension
Osteoporosis reduces bone density throughout the spine including cervical vertebrae
Canadian C-spine Rule mandates imaging: age >65, dangerous mechanism
Elderly patients may have limited range of motion at baseline, masking acute cervical injury
Concurrent anticoagulation increases risk of epidural hematoma if spinal fracture present

reasoning: This 81-year-old anticoagulated female with osteoporosis presenting after a fall with head strike represents a complex trauma assessment. While simple mechanical fall is statistically most likely, the clinical priority is driven by the high-consequence don't-miss diagnoses. Subdural hematoma ranks second at 0.25 probability — not because it is the most likely diagnosis, but because the combination of warfarin anticoagulation and head trauma creates a well-documented high-risk scenario where delayed diagnosis is frequently fatal. The lucid interval phenomenon means current GCS 15 provides no reassurance. Hip fracture at 0.20 is highly plausible given the demographics and must be systematically excluded including with MRI if X-rays are negative and clinical suspicion persists. Cardiac syncope as the underlying cause of the fall must be explored — the distinction between 'fall causing dizziness' and 'dizziness causing fall' has fundamental implications for management and recurrence prevention. C-spine fracture is a don't-miss given mechanism, age, and osteoporosis.
